title: Charcoal-Grilled Salmon with Forest Herb Oil
description: Salmon seared over high heat with a binchotan-inspired char, served with a wild herb oil of shiso, chives, and parsley. Inspired by Narisawa's reverence for fire and forest.
instructions: Blend shiso, chives, parsley, olive oil, lemon juice, lemon zest, and garlic in a small blender until smooth. Season herb oil with a pinch of salt and set aside.
Pat salmon fillets dry. Rub with avocado oil, sea salt, black pepper, and optional charcoal powder for a dark crust effect.
Heat a cast-iron skillet or grill to very high heat. Sear salmon skin-side down for 4 minutes until the skin is deeply crisp.
Flip and cook 3-4 minutes more for medium, adjusting for thickness.
Rest salmon 2 minutes. Spoon forest herb oil generously over each fillet and serve.

title: Soil-Crusted Beef Tenderloin with Root Vegetables
description: Beef tenderloin coated in an earthy spice crust of mushroom powder, black sesame, and smoked paprika — evoking Narisawa's iconic 'Soil Soup' and his dialogue between earth and plate.
instructions: Preheat oven to 425°F. Toss beets, parsnips, and carrots with 1 tbsp avocado oil, thyme, and a pinch of salt. Spread on a sheet pan and roast for 20 minutes.
Mix mushroom powder, ground black sesame, smoked paprika, 1 tsp salt, and pepper in a bowl.
Pat beef dry, rub with 1 tbsp avocado oil, then press the soil spice crust mixture all over the surface.
Heat remaining 1 tbsp avocado oil in a cast-iron skillet over high heat. Sear beef on all sides, about 2 minutes per side.
Transfer seared beef to the sheet pan with root vegetables. Roast 15-18 minutes for medium-rare (internal temp 130°F).
Rest beef 10 minutes, then slice. Arrange over roasted root vegetables and serve.

title: Kombu Dashi-Poached Cod with Bamboo Shoots
description: Cod gently poached in a rich kombu and bonito dashi with bamboo shoots and shiitake. Narisawa's ocean-meets-forest philosophy, rendered clean and simple.
instructions: Make dashi: bring water and kombu to a gentle simmer over medium heat (do not boil). Remove kombu just before boiling. Add bonito flakes, steep 3 minutes, then strain through a fine-mesh sieve.
Return dashi to pot. Add coconut aminos, mirin, ginger coins, and salt. Bring to a gentle simmer.
Add shiitake mushrooms and bamboo shoots, simmer 5 minutes.
Season cod with a pinch of salt. Gently lower fillets into the simmering dashi. Poach at a bare simmer for 8-10 minutes until cod flakes easily.
Ladle cod into shallow bowls with broth, mushrooms, and bamboo shoots. Garnish with sliced green onions.

title: Forest Floor Chicken with Wild Mushrooms and Herbs
description: Roasted chicken thighs nestled among a medley of wild mushrooms, fresh herbs, and pine nuts — a tribute to Narisawa's satoyama walks through the Japanese forest floor.

ingredients.name: mixed wild mushrooms
ingredients.notes: maitake, shiitake, oyster — torn into pieces

instructions: Preheat oven to 425°F. Season chicken thighs generously with salt and pepper.
Heat avocado oil in a large oven-safe skillet over medium-high heat. Sear chicken skin-side down for 6-7 minutes until golden and crisp. Remove and set aside.
In the same skillet, add mushrooms, garlic, rosemary, and thyme. Cook 4-5 minutes, stirring, until mushrooms begin to brown. Drizzle with coconut aminos.
Nestle chicken thighs skin-side up on top of the mushroom bed. Transfer skillet to oven.
Roast 20-25 minutes until chicken reaches 175°F internal temperature and skin is deeply golden.
Scatter toasted pine nuts over the dish. Serve directly from the skillet.

title: Black Garlic Strip Steak with Charred Scallions
description: Seared strip steak finished with a black garlic and coconut aminos glaze, served with charred scallions. The deep umami of black garlic channels Narisawa's mastery of fermentation and fire.
instructions: Bring steaks to room temperature 30 minutes before cooking. Season generously with salt and pepper on all sides.
Mix black garlic paste with coconut aminos, rice vinegar, and sesame oil to form a glaze.
Heat avocado oil in a heavy skillet over high heat until smoking. Sear steaks 4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
In the last minute, spoon the black garlic glaze over the steaks and baste with pan juices.
Remove steaks to a cutting board to rest. In the same hot skillet, char the whole scallions for 2-3 minutes, turning once, until blistered and softened.
Slice steaks against the grain. Serve topped with any remaining pan glaze and charred scallions alongside.

title: Ocean Trout with Nori Butter and Wakame Salad
description: Pan-seared trout with a nori-infused coconut oil finish, paired with a refreshing wakame seaweed salad. Narisawa's deep respect for the ocean, adapted dairy-free.
instructions: Toss rehydrated wakame with sliced cucumber, rice vinegar, sesame oil, sesame seeds, and a pinch of salt. Refrigerate while cooking fish.
Melt 2 tbsp coconut oil in a skillet over medium-high heat. Season trout with salt and white pepper.
Sear trout skin-side down for 4 minutes until skin is crisp. Flip, cook 2-3 minutes more.
Remove trout to plates. Reduce heat to low, add remaining 1 tbsp coconut oil and crumbled nori to the skillet. Stir until nori is fragrant and oil is infused, about 30 seconds.
Spoon nori oil over each trout fillet. Serve with wakame-cucumber salad and lemon wedges.

title: Tea-Smoked Duck Breast with Kabocha Purée
description: Duck breast lightly smoked with hojicha tea leaves, served over a silky kabocha squash purée. Narisawa's balance of smoke, sweetness, and precision in every slice.

ingredients.name: hojicha tea leaves
ingredients.notes: or lapsang souchong for smokier flavor

instructions: Steam or boil kabocha cubes until very tender, about 15 minutes. Drain and mash with coconut cream, grated ginger, nutmeg, and a pinch of salt until smooth. Keep warm.
Season duck breasts with salt and pepper. Place skin-side down in a cold skillet, then turn heat to medium. Render fat slowly for 10-12 minutes until skin is very crisp.
Flip duck and cook 4-5 minutes for medium-rare (135°F internal). Remove and rest on a cutting board.
Optional tea-smoke: line a wok with foil, add tea leaves and rice. Set a rack above, place seared duck on rack, cover tightly. Heat over medium 3-4 minutes to lightly smoke.
Slice duck breast thinly on the bias. Spoon kabocha purée onto plates, fan duck slices over top, and garnish with chives.

title: White Miso-Glazed Lamb Chops with Sansho Pepper
description: Lamb chops marinated in white miso and mirin, seared until caramelized, finished with sansho pepper. Narisawa's Japanese-French fusion at its most elegant.
instructions: Whisk together white miso, mirin, coconut aminos, ginger, and garlic. Coat lamb chops with the mixture, marinate 15 minutes (or up to 4 hours refrigerated).
Scrape off excess marinade (reserve it). Pat chops lightly dry. Season with salt.
Heat avocado oil in a heavy skillet over high heat. Sear lamb chops 3 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Reduce heat to medium. Add reserved marinade to the pan, let it bubble and caramelize around the chops for 30 seconds per side.
Transfer to plates, dust with sansho pepper, and garnish with sliced green onions.

title: Bamboo Forest Chicken in Aromatic Broth
description: Tender chicken simmered in a clear aromatic broth with bamboo shoots, enoki mushrooms, and yuzu. Narisawa's bamboo forest walks distilled into a nourishing bowl.
instructions: Bring chicken bone broth to a simmer with ginger slices. Let ginger infuse for 5 minutes.
Add bamboo shoots and coconut aminos. Simmer 5 minutes.
Add sliced chicken breast. Poach gently at a bare simmer for 6-8 minutes until cooked through.
Add enoki mushrooms in the last 2 minutes of cooking.
Remove ginger slices. Season with salt, white pepper, and yuzu juice.
Ladle into deep bowls. Garnish with fresh cilantro leaves.

title: Charred Brassicas with Sliced Beef and Ponzu
description: Deeply charred broccoli and broccolini alongside thin-sliced seared beef, dressed in a bright citrus ponzu. Narisawa's celebration of vegetables elevated to co-star with the protein.
instructions: Make ponzu: whisk together coconut aminos, lemon juice, orange juice, and rice vinegar. Set aside.

Heat 2 tbsp avocado oil in a large skillet over high heat. Add broccoli and broccolini in a single layer, pressing down. Char without moving for 3-4 minutes. Flip, char 2 more minutes. Season with salt, add garlic, toss 30 seconds. Transfer to a platter.
Season sirloin with salt and pepper. Add remaining 1 tbsp oil to the same screaming-hot skillet. Sear steak 3-4 minutes per side for medium-rare.
Rest steak 5 minutes, then slice thin against the grain.
Arrange sliced beef over charred brassicas. Drizzle generously with ponzu and sprinkle with sesame seeds.

title: Salmon with Mountain Vegetables and Sansho Salt
description: Crispy-skinned salmon served over sautéed mountain vegetables — fiddleheads, asparagus, and ramps — with sansho salt. Narisawa's spring sansai tradition.
instructions: Mix sansho pepper with flaky sea salt to create sansho salt. Set aside.
Heat avocado oil in a large skillet over medium-high heat. Season salmon with a pinch of sansho salt. Place skin-side down, press gently. Sear 5 minutes until skin is deeply crisp.
Flip salmon, cook 3 minutes more. Remove to a plate.
In the same skillet, add olive oil. Sauté fiddleheads (or snap peas) and asparagus 3 minutes. Add ramps, cook 1 minute more until just wilted.
Divide mountain vegetables among plates. Top with salmon. Squeeze lemon over everything and finish with a generous pinch of sansho salt.

title: Seared Venison with Beet Reduction and Watercress
description: Pan-seared venison loin with a deep ruby beet reduction and peppery watercress. Narisawa's earth-to-plate philosophy, where the soil's color becomes the sauce.
instructions: Make beet reduction: simmer beet juice with shallot, red wine vinegar, coconut aminos, and thyme sprigs over medium heat until reduced to about 1/4 cup and syrupy, about 15 minutes. Strain and keep warm.
Season venison generously with salt and cracked pepper. Let sit at room temperature 15 minutes.
Heat avocado oil in a cast-iron skillet over high heat. Sear venison on all sides, about 2 minutes per side for medium-rare (130°F internal).
Rest venison 8 minutes, then slice into medallions.
Lightly dress watercress with olive oil and a pinch of salt. Arrange on plates.
Fan venison medallions over watercress, drizzle beet reduction over and around the plate.

title: Shiitake-Stuffed Delicata Squash with Ground Turkey
description: Roasted delicata squash halves filled with a savory mixture of ground turkey, shiitake mushrooms, and water chestnuts. Narisawa's ethos of honoring each ingredient's natural form.
instructions: Preheat oven to 400°F. Brush squash halves with 1 tbsp avocado oil, season with salt and pepper. Roast cut-side down on a sheet pan for 20 minutes until just tender.
While squash roasts, heat remaining avocado oil in a skillet over medium-high heat. Cook ground turkey, breaking it up, until browned — about 6 minutes.
Add shiitake mushrooms, garlic, and ginger. Cook 4 minutes until mushrooms are softened.
Stir in water chestnuts, coconut aminos, and sesame oil. Cook 2 minutes more. Season with salt and pepper.
Flip roasted squash cut-side up. Fill each half generously with the turkey-mushroom mixture.
Return to oven for 10 minutes to heat through. Garnish with green onions and serve.

title: Matcha-Crusted Seared Tuna with Daikon
description: Ahi tuna seared in a vibrant matcha and black sesame crust, served with grated daikon and a citrus-aminos dipping sauce. Narisawa's reverence for tea culture meets the ocean.
instructions: Mix matcha powder, black sesame seeds, white sesame seeds, and salt on a plate.
Make dipping sauce: combine coconut aminos, lime juice, and grated ginger in a small bowl.
Pat tuna steaks very dry. Press all sides firmly into the matcha-sesame mixture to coat.
Heat avocado oil in a skillet over highest heat until just smoking. Sear tuna 30-45 seconds per side for rare (or longer to preference). Edges should be seared, center raw.
Slice tuna into 1/4-inch slices. Arrange on plates with a mound of grated daikon. Serve dipping sauce alongside.

title: Root Vegetable-Braised Short Ribs with Burdock
description: Beef short ribs slow-braised with burdock root, turnips, and shiitake in a coconut aminos-mirin broth. Narisawa's deep-earth cooking — patient, elemental, transformative.

ingredients.name: burdock root
ingredients.notes: peeled, sliced on bias — or substitute salsify or parsnip

instructions: Preheat oven to 325°F. Season short ribs generously with salt and pepper.
Heat avocado oil in a Dutch oven over high heat. Sear short ribs on all sides until deeply browned, about 8 minutes total. Remove and set aside.
Reduce heat to medium. Add onion, garlic, and ginger to the pot. Cook 3 minutes, stirring.
Add coconut aminos, mirin, and bone broth. Scrape up any fond from the bottom. Bring to a simmer.
Return short ribs to the pot. Add burdock root, turnips, and shiitake mushrooms around the ribs.
Cover and braise in the oven for 2.5-3 hours until the meat is fall-off-the-bone tender.
Remove ribs and vegetables to a serving dish. Skim fat from braising liquid, then reduce on stovetop 5 minutes. Spoon sauce over ribs, garnish with green onions.

title: Whole Roasted Branzino with Yuzu and Fresh Herbs
description: Whole branzino stuffed with yuzu slices, shiso, and ginger, roasted until the skin crackles. Narisawa's respect for the whole animal — head to tail, nothing wasted.

ingredients.name: whole branzino
ingredients.notes: about 1 lb each, cleaned and scaled

ingredients.name: yuzu
ingredients.notes: thinly sliced — or substitute Meyer lemon

instructions: Preheat oven to 450°F. Line a sheet pan with parchment paper.
Score each branzino with 3 diagonal slashes on each side. Season inside and out with salt and white pepper.
Stuff each cavity with yuzu slices, shiso leaves, and ginger slices.
Rub the outside of each fish with avocado oil. Place on the prepared sheet pan.
Roast 20-25 minutes until the skin is golden and crisp and the flesh flakes easily near the bone.
Drizzle with olive oil, scatter chives over the top, and serve with lemon wedges.

title: Forest Mushroom and Soft Egg Rice Bowl
description: A donburi of mixed forest mushrooms sautéed with garlic and coconut aminos, laid over rice with a soft-cooked egg. Narisawa's humble satoyama comfort — foraged simplicity at its best.

ingredients.name: mixed mushrooms
ingredients.notes: shiitake, maitake, king oyster — sliced or torn

instructions: Bring a pot of water to a boil. Gently lower eggs in, cook exactly 6.5 minutes for a jammy yolk. Transfer to ice water.
Heat avocado oil in a large skillet over high heat. Add mushrooms in a single layer, do not stir for 3 minutes to get a sear. Stir, cook 3 more minutes.
Add garlic, cook 1 minute. Add coconut aminos and mirin, toss to coat. Cook 2 minutes until glossy. Drizzle with sesame oil and season with salt.
Divide warm rice among 4 bowls. Top with sautéed mushrooms.
Peel eggs and halve. Place one egg on each bowl. Garnish with green onions and shichimi togarashi.
